title: The Church-Warden And The Curate
content:
I.
Eh? good daï¿½y! good daï¿½y! thaw it beï¿½nï¿½t not mooch of a daï¿½y,
Nasty, casselty (1) weather! anï¿½ mea Haï¿½fe down wiï¿½ my haï¿½y! (2)

II.
How be the farm gittin on? noï¿½ways. Gittin on iï¿½deeï¿½d!
Why, tonups was Haï¿½fe on ï¿½em fingers anï¿½ toas, (3) anï¿½ the mare brokken-kneeï¿½d,
Anï¿½ pigs didnï¿½t sell at fall, (4) anï¿½ wa lost wer Haldeny cow,
Anï¿½ it beï¿½ts ma to knaw wot she died on, but woolï¿½s looking oop ony how.

III.
Anï¿½ soï¿½ theyï¿½ve maï¿½de tha a parson, anï¿½ thouï¿½ll git along, niver fear,
Fur I beï¿½n chuch-warden mysen iï¿½ the parish fur fifteen year.
Wellï¿½sin ther beï¿½ chuch-wardens, ther mun be parsons anï¿½ all,
Anï¿½ if tï¿½ï¿½ne stick alongside tï¿½uther (5) the chuch weï¿½nt happen a fall.

IV.
Fur I wur a Baptis wonst, anï¿½ ageï¿½n the toithe anï¿½ the raï¿½te,
Till I fun (6) that it warnï¿½t not the gaï¿½inist (7) waï¿½y to the narra Gaï¿½te.
Anï¿½ I canï¿½t abeï¿½r ï¿½em, I canï¿½t, fur a lot on ï¿½em coomï¿½d ta-year (8)ï¿½
I wur down wiï¿½ the rheumatis thenï¿½to my pond to wesh thessens theereï¿½
Sa I sticks like the ivin (9) as long as I lives to the owd chuch now,
Fur they weshï¿½d their sins iï¿½ my pond, anï¿½ I doubts they poisonï¿½d the cow.

V.
Ay, anï¿½ ya seed the Bishop. They sayï¿½s ï¿½at he coomï¿½d fra nowtï¿½
Burn iï¿½ traï¿½de. Sa I warrants ï¿½e niver said haï¿½fe wot ï¿½e thowt,
But ï¿½e creeï¿½pt anï¿½ ï¿½e crawlï¿½d along, till ï¿½e feeï¿½ld ï¿½e could howd ï¿½is oï¿½n,
Then ï¿½e married a greï¿½t Yerlï¿½s darter, anï¿½ sits oï¿½ the Bishopï¿½s throan.

VI.
Now Iï¿½ll gie the a bit oï¿½ my mind anï¿½ tha weant be taakinï¿½ offence,
Fur thou be a big scholard now wiï¿½ a hoonderd haï¿½cre oï¿½ senseï¿½
But sich an obstropulous (10) ladï¿½naay, naayï¿½fur I minds tha sa well,
Thaï¿½d niver not hopple (11) thy tongue, anï¿½ the tongueï¿½s sit afire oï¿½ Hell,
As I says to my missis to-daï¿½y, when she hurlï¿½d a plaï¿½te at the cat
Anï¿½ anoother ageï¿½n my noï¿½se. Ya was niver sa bad as that.

VII.
But I minds when iï¿½ Howlaby beck won daï¿½y ya was ticklinï¿½ oï¿½ trout,
Anï¿½ keeï¿½per ï¿½e seed ya an roonï¿½d, anï¿½ ï¿½e bealï¿½d (12) to ya ï¿½Lad coom houtï¿½
Anï¿½ ya stood oop naï¿½kt iï¿½ the beck, anï¿½ ya tellï¿½d ï¿½im to knaw his awn plaï¿½ce
Anï¿½ ye callï¿½d ï¿½im a clown, ya did, anï¿½ ya thrawï¿½d the fish iï¿½ ï¿½is faï¿½ce,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e tornï¿½d (13) as red as a stag-tuckeyï¿½s (14) wattles, but theer anï¿½ then
I coï¿½mbï¿½d ï¿½im down, fur I promised yaï¿½d niver not do it ageï¿½n.

VIII.
Anï¿½ I cotchï¿½d tha wonst iï¿½ my garden, when thou was a height-year-howd, (15)
Anï¿½ I fun thy pockets as full oï¿½ my pippins as iver theyï¿½d ï¿½owd, (16)
Anï¿½ thou was as peï¿½rky (17) as owt, anï¿½ tha maï¿½de me as mad as mad,
But I says to the ï¿½keeï¿½p ï¿½em, anï¿½ welcomeï¿½ fur thou was the Parsonï¿½s lad.

IX.
An Parson ï¿½e ï¿½ears on it all, anï¿½ then taï¿½kes kindly to me,
Anï¿½ then I wur chose Chuch-warden anï¿½ coomï¿½d to the top oï¿½ the tree,
Fur Quolotyï¿½s hall my friends, anï¿½ they maï¿½kes ma a help to the poor,
When I gits the plaï¿½te fuller oï¿½ Soondays nor ony chuch-warden afoor,
Fur if iver thy feytherï¿½ed riled me I kepï¿½ mysen meeï¿½k as a lamb,
Anï¿½ saw by the Graï¿½ce oï¿½ the Lord, Mr. Harry, I ham wot I ham.

X.
But Parson ï¿½e will speï¿½k out, saw, now ï¿½e be sixty-seven,
Heï¿½ll niver swap Owlby anï¿½ Scratby fur owt but the Kingdom oï¿½ Heaven:
Anï¿½ thouï¿½II be ï¿½is Curate ï¿½ere, but, if iver tha meï¿½ns to git ï¿½igher,
The mun tackle the sins oï¿½ the Woï¿½ld, (18) anï¿½ not the faults oï¿½ the Squire.
Anï¿½ I reckons thaï¿½ll light of a livinï¿½ some-wheers iï¿½ the Wowd (19) or the Fen,
If tha cottons down to thy betters, anï¿½ keeï¿½ps thysen to thysen.
But niver not speï¿½k plaï¿½in out, if tha wants to git forrards a bit,
But creeï¿½p along the hedge-bottoms, anï¿½ thouï¿½ll be a Bishop yit.

XI.
Naï¿½y, but tha mun speï¿½k hout to the Baptises here iï¿½ the town,
Fur moï¿½st on ï¿½em talks ageï¿½n tithe, anï¿½ Iï¿½d like the to preï¿½ch ï¿½em down,
Fur theyï¿½ve bin a-preï¿½chinï¿½ mea down, they heve, anï¿½ I haï¿½tes ï¿½em now,
Fur they leï¿½ved their nasty sins iï¿½ my pond, anï¿½ it poisonï¿½d the cow.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
